content:
# Luke Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 97-99 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_009_page_001.jpg, LUK_009_page_002.jpg, LUK_009_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** And having called together the twelve, he gave to them power and authority over all the demons, and to heal diseases.

**2.** And he sent them to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the [ones] being sick.

**3.** And he said to them, "Take nothing for the way, neither staff nor bag nor bread nor silver, nor to have two tunics each.

**4.** And into whatever house you enter, there remain, and from there go out.

**5.** And as many as might not receive you, going out from that city, shake off the dust from your feet for a testimony against them."

**6.** And going out, they were going through village by village, proclaiming-good-news and healing everywhere.

**7.** And Herod the tetrarch heard [of] all the [things] happening, and he was perplexed, on account of it being said by some that John has been raised from [the] dead,

**8.** and by some that Elijah had appeared, and by others that a certain prophet of the ancient [ones] had risen up.

**9.** And Herod said, "John I myself beheaded; but who is this concerning whom I hear such [things]?" And he was seeking to see him.

**10.** And the apostles, having returned, recounted to him what things they had done. And having taken them along, he withdrew privately into a city called Bethsaida.

**11.** But the crowds, having known [this], followed him. And having received them, he was speaking to them concerning the kingdom of God, and those having need of healing he was curing.

**12.** And the day began to decline. And the twelve, having come to [him], said to him, "Send away the crowd, so that, having gone into the surrounding villages and countryside, they might lodge and find provisions, because here we are in a desolate place."

**13.** But he said to them, "You yourselves give to them [something] to eat." And they said, "There are not to us more than five loaves and two fish, unless we ourselves, having gone, should buy food for all this people."

**14.** For there were about five thousand men. And he said to his disciples, "Make them recline in groups of about fifty each."

**15.** And they did so, and made all [of them] recline.

**16.** And having taken the five loaves and the two fish, having looked up into the heaven, he blessed them and broke [them] and was giving [them] to the disciples to set before the crowd.

**17.** And they ate and were all satisfied. And there was taken up what was left over to them — twelve baskets of fragments.

**18.** And it came to pass, while he was praying alone, the disciples were with him, and he questioned them, saying, "Who do the crowds say me to be?"

**19.** And they, having answered, said, "John the Baptist; but others, Elijah; and others, that a certain prophet of the ancient [ones] has risen up."

**20.** And he said to them, "But you, who do you say me to be?" And Peter, having answered, said, "The Christ of God."

**21.** And he, having strictly warned them, commanded [them] to tell this to no one,

**22.** saying, "It is necessary [for] the Son of Man to suffer many [things] and to be rejected by the elders and chief priests and scribes, and to be killed, and on the third day to be raised."

**23.** And he was saying to all, "If anyone wishes to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily, and follow me.

**24.** For whoever wishes to save his life shall lose it; but whoever loses his life on account of me, this [one] shall save it.

**25.** For what is a man profited, having gained the whole world, but having destroyed or having forfeited himself?

**26.** For whoever is ashamed of me and of my words, of this [one] the Son of Man shall be ashamed, whenever he comes in his glory and [the glory] of the Father and of the holy angels.

**27.** But I say to you truly, there are some of those standing here who shall not taste death until they see the kingdom of God."

**28.** And it came to pass, about eight days after these words, [that] having taken along Peter and John and James, he went up into the mountain to pray.

**29.** And it came to pass, while he was praying, the appearance of his face [became] different, and his clothing [became] white, flashing like lightning.

**30.** And behold, two men were conversing with him, who were Moses and Elijah,

**31.** who, having appeared in glory, were speaking [of] his departure, which he was about to fulfill in Jerusalem.

**32.** But Peter and those with him were having been weighed down with sleep. And having fully awakened, they saw his glory and the two men standing with him.

**33.** And it came to pass, as they were departing from him, Peter said to Jesus, "Master, it is good for us to be here, and let us make three tents, one for you and one for Moses and one for Elijah," not knowing what he was saying.

**34.** And [while] he [was] saying these [things], a cloud came and was overshadowing them; and they were afraid as they entered into the cloud.

**35.** And a voice came out of the cloud, saying, "This is my Son, the Chosen [One]; hear him."

**36.** And when the voice came, Jesus was found alone. And they kept silent and reported to no one in those days anything of what they had seen.

**37.** And it came to pass on the next day, [when] they came down from the mountain, a great crowd met him.

**38.** And behold, a man from the crowd cried out, saying, "Teacher, I beg you to look upon my son, because he is my only-born.

**39.** And behold, a spirit takes him, and suddenly he cries out, and it convulses him with foam, and with difficulty departs from him, crushing him.

**40.** And I begged your disciples that they might cast it out, and they were not able."

**41.** And Jesus, having answered, said, "O faithless and having been perverted generation, until when shall I be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here."

**42.** And while he was still approaching, the demon threw him down and convulsed [him]. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit and healed the boy and gave him back to his father.

**43.** And all were being astonished at the majesty of God. And [while] all were marveling at all the [things] which he was doing, he said to his disciples,

**44.** "You yourselves place into your ears these words: for the Son of Man is about to be delivered into [the] hands of men."

**45.** But they did not understand this saying, and it was having been hidden from them, so that they might not perceive it; and they were afraid to ask him concerning this saying.

**46.** And a reasoning entered among them, [as to] who might be [the] greatest of them.

**47.** And Jesus, having known the reasoning of their heart, having taken hold of a little child, set it beside himself,

**48.** and said to them, "Whoever receives this little child in my name receives me; and whoever receives me receives the [One] having sent me. For the [one] being least among all of you, this [one] is great."

**49.** And John, having answered, said, "Master, we saw someone in your name casting out demons, and we were forbidding him, because he does not follow with us."

**50.** And Jesus said to him, "Do not forbid [him]; for the [one] who is not against you is for you."

**51.** And it came to pass, when the days of his being-taken-up were being fulfilled, and he himself set his face to go into Jerusalem.

**52.** And he sent messengers before his face. And having gone, they entered into a village of [the] Samaritans, so as to make ready for him.

**53.** And they did not receive him, because his face was going into Jerusalem.

**54.** And the disciples James and John, having seen, said, "Lord, do you want [that] we should tell fire to come down from the heaven and consume them?"

**55.** But having turned, he rebuked them.

**56.** And they went into a different village.

**57.** And [as] they were going on the way, someone said to him, "I will follow you wherever you go."

**58.** And Jesus said to him, "The foxes have dens, and the birds of the heaven [have] nests, but the Son of Man has not where he might lay [his] head."

**59.** And he said to another, "Follow me." But he said, "Lord, permit me, having gone away first, to bury my father."

**60.** And he said to him, "Leave the dead to bury their own dead; but you, having gone away, proclaim the kingdom of God."

**61.** And another also said, "I will follow you, Lord; but first permit me to say farewell to those in my house."

**62.** And Jesus said to him, "No one having put [his] hand upon [the] plow and looking to the [things] behind is fit for the kingdom of God."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **9:1 — "the twelve":** Sinaiticus reads τους δωδεκα ("the twelve") without further specification. Some manuscripts add "his disciples" or "apostles." The Lukan usage of "the twelve" here matches 8:1.

2. **9:3 — "neither staff" (μητε ραβδον):** Sinaiticus reads μητε ("neither"), prohibiting even a staff. This stands in tension with Mark 6:8, which uses εἰ μη ("except") to permit a staff. This is a genuine Synoptic difference preserved in Sinaiticus.

3. **9:7 — "has been raised" (ηγερθη):** Sinaiticus uses the aorist passive ηγερθη ("was raised"), which in context conveys the report that John "has been raised." The passive voice implies divine agency in the raising.

4. **9:10 — "Bethsaida":** Sinaiticus reads Βηθσαιδα. Some manuscripts read Βηθσαιδαν (accusative form). The city identification is consistent across major witnesses.

5. **9:17 — "twelve baskets":** Sinaiticus reads δωδεκα κοφινοι ("twelve baskets"). The term κοφινος refers to a wicker basket typically associated with Jewish practice, distinct from the σπυρις ("large basket") used in the feeding of the 4,000.

6. **9:18 — "while he was praying alone":** Sinaiticus reads εν τω ειναι αυτον προσευχομενον κατα μονας. This characteristically Lukan emphasis on Jesus at prayer before pivotal moments (cf. 3:21, 6:12, 9:28) is well preserved in the manuscript.

7. **9:20 — "The Christ of God" (τον χριστον του θεου):** Sinaiticus reads τον χριστον του θεου. This is Luke's distinctive form of Peter's confession. Compare Matthew 16:16 ("the Christ, the Son of the living God") and Mark 8:29 ("the Christ"). Sinaiticus preserves the shorter Lukan reading.

8. **9:22 — "on the third day to be raised":** Sinaiticus reads τη τριτη ημερα εγερθηναι, using the aorist passive infinitive. Luke consistently uses "on the third day" (τη τριτη ημερα) rather than Mark's "after three days" (μετα τρεις ημερας).

9. **9:23 — "daily" (καθ ημεραν):** Sinaiticus includes καθ ημεραν ("daily"), a distinctly Lukan addition not found in the Markan parallel (Mk 8:34). This transforms the saying from a single act to a continuous practice.

10. **9:26 — "his glory and [the glory] of the Father and of the holy angels":** Sinaiticus reads τη δοξη αυτου και του πατρος και των αγιων αγγελων. The single article τη governs all three — his glory, the Father's, and the angels' — linking them in a single manifestation. Some witnesses add εν before each noun.

11. **9:28 — "about eight days" (ωσει ημεραι οκτω):** Sinaiticus reads ωσει ημεραι οκτω, approximately "eight days." Matthew 17:1 and Mark 9:2 both read "after six days" (μεθ ημερας εξ). Luke's inclusive reckoning counts both endpoints, yielding the same interval by different counting conventions.

12. **9:28 — "Peter and John and James":** Sinaiticus preserves the Lukan order placing John before James, consistent with 8:51. This differs from the Markan order "Peter and James and John" (Mk 9:2) and reflects Luke's special regard for John in Luke-Acts.

13. **9:29 — "different" (ετερον):** Sinaiticus reads ετερον ("different/other") for the appearance of his face, rather than the verb μετεμορφωθη ("was transfigured") used by Matthew and Mark. Luke avoids the term "transfigure" (μεταμορφοω), possibly to avoid pagan metamorphosis associations.

14. **9:29 — "flashing like lightning" (εξαστραπτων):** Sinaiticus reads εξαστραπτων, a vivid participle meaning "flashing forth as lightning." This is a uniquely Lukan term (also at 17:24 in variant form), replacing Mark's description of garments "glistening exceedingly white."

15. **9:31 — "departure" (εξοδον):** Sinaiticus reads εξοδον ("exodus/departure"), a theologically loaded term. It simultaneously denotes Jesus' death and evokes the Exodus of Israel. This conversation between Moses, Elijah, and Jesus about an "exodus" in Jerusalem is deeply typological.

16. **9:32 — "were having been weighed down with sleep":** Sinaiticus reads ησαν βεβαρημενοι υπνω, a periphrastic pluperfect construction (imperfect of ειμι + perfect passive participle). This conveys the state of having been progressively weighed down — preserved literally in the translation.

17. **9:35 — "the Chosen [One]" (ο εκλελεγμενος):** Sinaiticus reads ο εκλελεγμενος ("the one having been chosen/the Chosen One"), a perfect passive participle. This is a significant variant: most later manuscripts read ο αγαπητος ("the beloved"), harmonizing with the Matthean/Markan parallels and with the Baptism account (Lk 3:22). The Sinaiticus reading echoes Isaiah 42:1 (ο εκλεκτος μου) and is considered the more difficult — and likely more original — reading.

18. **9:38 — "only-born" (μονογενης):** Sinaiticus reads μονογενης, the same term used of Jesus in John 1:14, 18; 3:16. Here it describes the man's only son, amplifying the pathos. Luke uses this term three times for human children (7:12, 8:42, 9:38).

19. **9:41 — "having been perverted" (διεστραμμενη):** Sinaiticus reads the perfect passive participle διεστραμμενη ("having been twisted/perverted"), indicating a settled state of distortion. This echoes Deuteronomy 32:5 LXX (γενεα σκολια και διεστραμμενη), Moses' rebuke of Israel.

20. **9:44 — "place into your ears":** Sinaiticus reads θεσθε υμεις εις τα ωτα υμων, literally "you yourselves place into your ears." This emphatic Semitic idiom (cf. Exodus 17:14; 1 Samuel 8:21 LXX) stresses the importance of what follows. The aorist middle imperative θεσθε indicates urgency.

21. **9:45 — "it was having been hidden from them":** Sinaiticus reads ην παρακεκαλυμμενον απ αυτων, another periphrastic construction (imperfect + perfect passive participle), indicating a continuing state of concealment. The divine passive suggests God was hiding the meaning from them for the present.

22. **9:51 — "being-taken-up" (αναλημψεως):** Sinaiticus reads αναλημψεως, a noun denoting "taking up/assumption." This term encompasses not merely the Ascension but the entire complex of Jesus' departure — suffering, death, resurrection, and ascension. It marks the beginning of Luke's major "Travel Narrative" (9:51-19:44).

23. **9:51 — "he himself set his face":** Sinaiticus reads αυτος το προσωπον εστηρισεν, literally "he himself the face set firmly." This echoes Isaiah 50:7 LXX (εθηκα το προσωπον μου ως στερεαν πετραν — "I set my face as a firm rock"), connecting Jesus' resolve with the Suffering Servant.

24. **9:53 — "his face was going into Jerusalem":** Sinaiticus reads το προσωπον αυτου ην πορευομενον εις Ιερουσαλημ. This unusual periphrastic construction ("his face was going") extends the "set face" imagery from v. 51. The Samaritans reject him because his determined direction is toward Jerusalem, the rival worship site.

25. **9:54 — "James and John":** Sinaiticus reads Ιακωβος και Ιωαννης. Some later manuscripts add "as also Elijah did" (ως και Ηλιας εποιησεν), referencing 2 Kings 1:10-12. Sinaiticus lacks this addition, presenting the shorter text.

26. **9:55 — "he rebuked them":** Sinaiticus reads επετιμησεν αυτοις without the longer addition found in some manuscripts: "and said, 'You do not know of what spirit you are; for the Son of Man did not come to destroy the souls of men but to save [them].'" This significant addition is absent from Sinaiticus and is generally regarded as a later scribal expansion.

27. **9:57-62 — The three would-be followers:** Sinaiticus presents these as a unit of three encounters. The first person (v. 57) initiates; Jesus responds. The second (v. 59) is called by Jesus; the man requests delay. The third (v. 61) volunteers but with a condition. Sinaiticus identifies the speaker in v. 58 as Ιησους ("Jesus"), though some manuscripts read simply "he said."

28. **9:62 — "having put [his] hand upon [the] plow":** Sinaiticus reads επιβαλων την χειρα επ αροτρον. This saying is unique to Luke and has no Synoptic parallel. The agricultural metaphor recalls Elisha's call in 1 Kings 19:19-21, where Elisha was plowing when Elijah called him — but Jesus demands even greater immediacy than Elijah did.
